title: Coconut and sea salt bar soap
subtitle: A 100% coconut-oil bar with coarse sea salt, high-superfat formula
excerpt: A salt bar made from 100% coconut oil saponified at a high superfat of 20%. The high superfat compensates for coconut oil's stripping nature; the coarse sea salt gives a firm, dense bar with a fluffy lather and a gentle physical exfoliation.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), Ch. 5, coconut-oil properties and high-superfat bars. Kevin Dunn, Caveman Chemistry (2003), Ch. 9, lauric acid and lather structure. SoapCalc SAP-value tables (v2024): coconut oil NaOH SAP value 0.190.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 2 kg; use a larger mould. Salt bars harden very quickly, so pour into individual bar moulds rather than a loaf if doubling, which makes cutting optional.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Active work is 30 minutes. Salt bars harden and cure much faster than soft-oil bars; they are ready to use at 1-2 weeks rather than 4-6 weeks.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in a cool dry cupboard. Salt bars have a shorter shelf life than olive-oil bars because coconut oil, even at high superfat, has a higher iodine value. Store wrapped to prevent surface sweating.

recipeTools.notes: Pulse briefly only; salt bars trace very fast. You may not need the stick blender at all if the batch comes to trace quickly by hand-stirring.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oil left unsaponified. Salt bars use a higher superfat (18-20%) because coconut oil is naturally more cleansing and drying than olive oil; the extra unsaponified oil compensates.
The point where soap batter thickens to the consistency of warm custard. Salt bars reach trace very quickly because salt accelerates saponification; pour immediately at trace.
The resting period after unmoulding. Salt bars cure faster than soft-oil bars; 1-2 weeks is sufficient.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Salt bars harden within 24-48 hours, but can seize in the mould if left past 48 hours. Unmould at 48 hours and cut into 100 g bars. Salt bars are very hard at this stage; a wire cutter cuts cleanly without crumbling. Stand on a wire rack for a further 1-2 weeks before using.

projectSchedule.title: Bars are ready to use
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks in. The bars are cured and ready. They will be very hard and white, with a noticeably fluffy lather when wet. Store unwrapped in a dry cupboard.

content.text: A salt bar is 100% coconut oil soap with coarse sea salt added at trace. Coconut oil on its own makes a very cleansing, lathery bar that can be drying at a standard 5%
superfat

. Raising the superfat to 20% leaves enough unsaponified coconut oil to balance the cleansing action. The sea salt makes the bar extremely hard and fast-curing; it also produces a very dense, fluffy lather with a fine physical exfoliation from the salt crystals in the bar.

The lye calculation at 20% superfat: 800 g coconut oil × 0.190 = 152 g NaOH at 0% superfat. At 20% superfat: 152 × 0.80 = 121.6 g. Working weight: 122 g NaOH. Water at 33% of oil weight: 264 g distilled water.
What you need

items.prepNote: 76°F-melt-point coconut oil. 100% of the oil weight.
distilled or deionised. 33% of the oil weight.
99% pure cosmetic-grade NaOH at 20% superfat. Weigh to 0.1 g precision.
coarse sea salt. 50% of the oil weight; this is a high-salt bar. Fine table salt also works but produces a denser, less visually textured surface. Add at trace.

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Weigh 264 g distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add 122 g NaOH to the water in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never the reverse. The solution heats immediately and fumes briefly; do not lean over it. Stir until dissolved and clear, then set aside to cool to 38-42°C.
Melt the coconut oil

Weigh 800 g coconut oil into a saucepan and warm on a very low heat until melted. Remove from the heat and cool to 38-42°C. Weigh the 400 g of sea salt into a separate bowl and have it ready beside the mould.
Combine, add salt, pour immediately
When both phases are at 38-42°C, pour the lye into the coconut oil and stir briskly by hand. The batter may reach
trace

within 60-90 seconds without the stick blender; if not, give one short pulse on the blender. At trace, immediately tip in the sea salt and stir through with the silicone spatula with 15-20 strokes. The batter stiffens rapidly as the salt goes in; work quickly and pour into the mould as soon as the salt is incorporated.
Smooth the surface quickly. Do not tap the mould to settle, the batter is already setting. Leave uncovered at room temperature for 48 hours.
Cure: 1-2 weeks
Unmould at 48 hours and cut while the bar is still just firm enough to score cleanly. Salt bars
cure
in 1-2 weeks; they do not need the 4-6 week wait of soft-oil bars. The salt accelerates water evaporation and the bar is hard and mild quickly.
Patch test

Patch-test on the inside forearm for 24-48 hours. Even at 20% superfat, the high coconut-oil content makes this bar more cleansing than most; people with very dry skin may find it too stripping for the face. It is well-suited as a body or hand bar.
Storage and shelf life

12 months in a cool dry cupboard. Salt bars sweat slightly in humid conditions; wrap individually in paper or cling film to prevent surface moisture. The salt in the bar is a natural preservative that slows rancidity, but coconut oil still has a shorter shelf life than olive; check for a rancid smell after 12 months and discard any affected bar.
